one_line_takeaway: A DeepMind theory paper proves a new power-law test statistic for detecting Aaronson's Gumbel LLM watermark achieves near-optimal (matching within log factors) problem-dependent detection time among all model-agnostic watermarking schemes, but the authors' own informal tests show it underperforms the original exponential-statistic detector on actual language data.
key_finding: The proposed power-law detector achieves detection once n = Omega(log(n)log(1/delta)/beta) in a low-entropy example with a single rare token of probability beta (Example 10), versus n = Omega(log(n)log(1/delta)/beta^2) implied by subexponential statistics (including Aaronson's original exponential statistic) in the analogous rare-high-entropy setting (Example 9) -- i.e. a quadratic-to-linear improvement in the small-beta detection-time exponent for certain low-entropy distributions. However, the paper's own Discussion section candidly reports that in informal (unquantified) experiments, the power-law detector outperforms the exponential detector on synthetic examples matching the theory, but performs 'slightly worse... on language data,' attributed to extra logarithmic/constant factors -- so the asymptotic theoretical gain does not straightforwardly translate to observed practical gains on real language-model text.
core_contribution: The paper (Tor Lattimore, Google DeepMind) proposes a new detection statistic for the Gumbel watermarking scheme of Aaronson (2022), replacing Aaronson's exponential-statistic test with a truncated power-law statistic (Algorithm 3, Eq. 2-3). It proves a problem-dependent upper bound on detection time (Theorem 2: detection succeeds w.h.p. once sum_t G_eps(P_t) = Omega(sqrt(n log n log(1/delta))), where G_eps is a newly defined entropy-like quantity of the model's next-token distributions), and a nearly matching problem-dependent minimax lower bound (Theorem 20) that applies to ANY sequential watermarking scheme satisfying a general 'valid selection kernel' definition (Definition 12), not just Gumbel. Combined, these show the Gumbel scheme plus the new power-law detector is near-optimal (up to logarithmic factors) among all model-agnostic watermarking/detection schemes when the next-token distributions are i.i.d.-sampled from a symmetric distribution.
threat_model: This is not an adversarial-robustness paper and defines no adversary who tampers with, paraphrases, or otherwise attacks the watermark. The setting is a purely statistical one: a detector (who knows the secret key but not necessarily the model) must distinguish, from token sequences alone, whether the sequence was sampled honestly from the language model with the watermarking procedure (Algorithm 1) applied (alternative hypothesis P1) versus sampled/generated without it (null hypothesis P0). The only 'threat' modeled is a statistical false-positive/false-negative tradeoff (confidence level delta controls false-positive rate); there is no consideration of watermark removal, spoofing, or human-edited/mixed text, which the paper explicitly notes is addressed by other cited works (Li et al. 2025a) but not by this one.
limitations: No experiments on real language models or real text are reported with any quantitative results, datasets, or baselines -- the paper states this explicitly ('You surely noticed the absence of experiments') and only gives a qualitative, unquantified remark about performance on 'language data' being slightly worse than on synthetic examples. There is a logarithmic-factor gap between the proven upper bound (Theorem 2) and lower bound (Theorem 20) that the authors state is likely not closable for this specific statistic. The lower bound (Theorem 20) requires a symmetry assumption on the distribution xi over next-token distributions, and the near-optimality claim is relative only to a restricted class of 'sequential watermarking schemes' (Definition 12) that use i.i.d. uniform noise combined via a valid selection kernel -- it does not cover schemes with different noise structures. No robustness analysis against paraphrasing, editing, or other adversarial removal attacks is included (explicitly deferred to other cited work).

quantitative_results: Theorem 2 (upper bound): detection succeeds with P1-probability >= 1-2*delta once sum_{t=1}^n G_eps(P_t) >= C*tau, where tau = Theta(sqrt(n log(n) log(1/delta)))
Theorem 20 (lower bound): no test can achieve both error rates <= delta unless n >= min(1/(2 E[G_1(P_eps)]), log(1/(16 delta)) / (16 E[G_{1/2}(P_eps^c)]^2))
Example 8 (constant low entropy, p=(1-beta,beta)): detection once n = Omega(log(n)log(1/delta)/(beta(1-beta)))
Example 9 (rare high-entropy token, prob beta): detection once n = Omega(log(n)log(1/delta)/beta^2)
Example 10 (many rare tokens, prob beta/(d-1) each): detection once n = Omega(log(n)log(1/delta)/beta)
Aaronson (2022) baseline result cited: detection with exponential statistic occurs once n = Omega(log(1/delta)/H_bar^2), H_bar the average entropy
Discussion section: no numeric results given for 'language data' experiments -- only the qualitative statement that the power-law detector is 'slightly worse' there than the exponential detector
baselines_compared: Aaronson (2022) exponential-statistic detector for Gumbel watermarking (theoretically and briefly, qualitatively, empirically)
Dathathri et al. (2024) Gaussian/Bernoulli-convolution-based statistic (theoretical comparison only, re subexponential tail behavior)
